title: The “Should I See It in a Movie Theater?” Test: ‘Tron: Ares,’ ‘Roofman,’ and ‘After the Hunt.’ Plus, the Magic of ‘Mr. Scorsese.’
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins run three underperforming new releases ('Tron: Ares,' 'Roofman,' and 'After the Hunt') through a should-you-see-it-in-a-theater test, mark the death of Diane Keaton, and Sean talks to Rebecca Miller about her Scorsese documentary.

films.title: Reds
films.note: Sean's pick for her greatest performance: a complicated part opposite Jack Nicholson and Warren Beatty, the two alphas of the era, whom he says she blows off the screen.

films.title: The First Wives Club
films.note: Amanda's angle on the other Keaton, the one outside Letterboxd: the 'You Don't Own Me' scene with Goldie Hawn and Bette Midler that every woman she knows reposted, Keaton at the center of it.

films.title: Baby Boom
films.note: Nancy Meyers co-wrote and produced it. Amanda keeps returning to a story Meyers told about the airport scene, where Keaton, off-script, tucks the inherited baby under her arm like a football, a daffy choice that sums up the character.

films.title: Something's Gotta Give
films.note: Just celebrated on the show's 25-for-25 list. Emblem of the late-career run that made her a box-office star in her 50s, a rare third act after the ingenue and the industry-taste-leader phases.

segments.heading: What do people want at the movie theater?
segments.note: the box office and the test

films.title: Black Phone 2
films.note: One of the coming weekend's tests Sean flags: the sequel to the Ethan Hawke Blumhouse movie.

films.title: Good Fortune
films.note: The other test: Aziz Ansari's comedy with Keanu Reeves and Seth Rogen, two men familiar with theatrical success, which nobody seems to be talking about.

segments.heading: ‘Tron: Ares’
segments.note: the wow/FOMO, communal, and at-home test

films.title: Tron: Ares
films.note: Joachim Rønning's third Tron, the first since Legacy 15 years ago, with a Nine Inch Nails score Sean and Amanda agree is the best thing in it. Jared Leto (who has never opened a movie, and whom Sean watched get a shrug from his own fan screening) plays a nice-guy AI, an inverted Terminator arriving as every creative person is warning about AI. Amanda took a 10-minute nap and left to call her heating technician; Sean makes a narrow case for it high, in 3D or Dolby, for the light-cycle sequences. It made $33 million. Both no. Includes a Science Corner on the AI orange tree.

segments.heading: ‘Roofman’
segments.note: the movie they want to lift up

films.title: Roofman
films.note: Derek Cianfrance's first film they can discuss on the show, written with Kirt Gunn, based on the true story of Jeffrey Manchester, an army vet turned McDonald's robber who escapes prison and hides out in a Toys R Us. Channing Tatum in a loose, 70s-style character study; a deep bench of Kirsten Dunst, Ben Mendelsohn, Peter Dinklage, LaKeith Stanfield, Juno Temple, Uzo Aduba. Sean was grooving for an hour and loves the fuck-up-you-still-root-for strain (Catch Me If You Can, Dog Day Afternoon). Amanda bumped hard on the ending, arguing Tatum's easy charm never bridges to whatever compulsion drives the character. Both recommend it; a sold-out Tuesday matinee suggests it's finding people.

segments.heading: ‘After the Hunt’
segments.note: Luca, and the script that isn't there

films.title: After the Hunt
films.note: Luca Guadagnino's campus-politics movie, written by Nora Garrett, scored by Trent Reznor and Atticus Ross, shot by Malik Hassan Sayeed (his first feature in 27 years, after Belly). Julia Roberts as a Yale philosophy professor up for tenure whose colleague (Andrew Garfield) is accused of assault by her TA (Ayo Edebiri). Both loved the first hour (beautiful, wry, Woody-Allen-font knowing) and think Roberts is genuinely great. Then it slams on the brakes and turns deathly serious, and neither can say what the script is trying to say; the baffling ending and epilogue lost them. Debts to Crimes and Misdemeanors and Tár run all over it.

segments.heading: Rebecca Miller on ‘Mr. Scorsese’
segments.note: the five-hour portrait on Apple TV

films.title: Goodfellas
films.note: The first Scorsese film Miller ever saw. She came to him late, as a painter drawn to other kinds of movies, then went back through the earlier work.

films.title: Raging Bull
films.note: Named alongside The Age of Innocence as one of the films that opened the door for her once she started watching him again and again.

films.title: The Age of Innocence
films.note: A really important film for her in the conversion, cited with Raging Bull and Goodfellas.

films.title: Taxi Driver
films.note: Brian De Palma's warning to her that the feature-length plan was doomed: 'you're gonna get to Taxi Driver, you cannot do the whole thing.' He was right; it grew to five hours.

films.title: The Last Waltz
films.note: Sean's favorite sequence in the doc, built around one of Robbie Robertson's last interviews before his death: the purity of the impulse, and Scorsese unable to stop shooting even mid-studio-picture.

films.title: Shutter Island
films.note: Miller's example of how the doc weights films by how much life bled into them: it becomes important as the hinge around a semi-breakdown, part of the tango between the life and the art.

films.title: Personal Velocity
films.note: Miller's own voiceover-heavy film, which started the relationship: nervous about it, she asked Scorsese for reference points and he sent her 1940s English films, then gave notes. That became how they knew each other.

films.title: Marty Supreme
films.note: Miller's last-great-thing. She loved its energy and its message about masculinity, and felt the maker deep inside it.

referenced.group: The state of the box office
